data.title: Chord Energy: The Market Is Sleeping On One Of The Best Oil Opportunities Today
data.description:
Introduction

Since I last covered Chord Energy (CHRD), the company reported a very solid earnings report, highlighting solid cost efficiencies, a healthy balance sheet, and a 20% increase in FCF per share despite the lower oil prices, reiterating my Strong Buy rating.

While the market is trying to figure out what to believe about Big Tech’s AI overspending, Chord continues to go under the market’s radar, expanding and still producing massive amounts of money even during the current environment, offering an opportunity to get exposure to a hard commodity through low-cost, high-quality assets.

Internal Developments

Chord’s Q3 was solid overall, beating EPS expectations but missing the market’s revenue estimates, although what I’m paying more attention to is the huge adjusted free cash flow of ~$230 million in Q3, delivering free cash flow per growth of over 20% since February. This gets us to $590.85 million in free cash flow YTD, while they are expecting ~4% higher oil volumes and $100 million lower CAPEX next year.

Since they expect CAPEX of ~$1.365B at midpoint this year, annualizing the cash provided by operating activities, we’re looking at a free cash flow of roughly $816 million (not far from the $850 million they were expecting), which is extremely good for a ~$5.6 billion market cap, especially since we’re talking about high-quality US oil assets.

Again, their 4-mile lateral program plays a big role in this, helping them reduce their breakeven by $8 to $12 per barrel at the cost of higher CAPEX now, organically improving the quality of their inventory and extending its life.

Meanwhile, their production and LOE efficiencies, combined with capital, marketing, and other improvements, are expected to boost FY25’s free cash flow by about $115 million, which is massive for a ~$5.6 billion market cap company that’s already producing significant FCF, expecting these savings to continue into 2026+.

They also reiterated a point about the quality of the Williston Basin inventory acquired recently, saying that its breakeven in the $40s helped lower the weighted-average breakeven of CHRD as a whole. In terms of business quality, this acquisition helps fortify the idea of how different Chord is today compared to its parents (Oasis and Whiting), even half a decade ago, when they both went through financial restructurings.

Yes, both of them filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy back in 2020 due to the pandemic shock, but I’m investing in CHRD for its bright future, not for its past - not to mention that the combined company changed significantly following the bankruptcy filing and subsequent portfolio/balance sheet adjustments.

Financially, based on CHRD’s latest report, we continue to see a very solid position, with the current assets covering the current liabilities and not a lot of debt despite increasing it to buy the portfolio mentioned earlier. This solid financial position comes alongside significant buybacks in recent history, while the pro forma free cash flow per share is up more than 35% since Chord announced the Enerplus transaction back in February 2024.

And all of this comes as the company maintains a very solid ~5.3% dividend plus massive buybacks, with a very solid distribution policy that takes into account the company’s leverage (currently at 0.6x net debt to LTM Adjusted EBITDA), aiming at preserving their financial health above all, while still paying a very solid yield at current levels.

Macro Developments

Probably the biggest and still developing matter is the US-Venezuela situation, so I’ll begin with that. For context, this began as a “war on drugs” initiative, and the US escalated things by placing an aircraft carrier in the Caribbean, proceeding to strike dozens of ships, while airlines canceled Venezuela flights, followed by several more warnings alongside threats.

As expected, Trump’s recent move to seize a Venezuelan tanker (the most recent development at the time of writing this) sparked blockade fears for the market, making most oil stocks go up as a result. The way I see this, a potential attack on Venezuela can cause massive ripples in the short- and medium-term not only in the oil market, but also potentially causing significant disruptions in the region’s trade with the US as a whole, as it can pose a risk for trading routes.

We’ve also seen US fighter jets reportedly entering Venezuelan airspace for 40 minutes, while Colombia's president said what seems like the obvious fact that this pressure is about oil, not drugs, reiterating what probably everyone is aware of. We’ve seen Ukraine also claiming that a top general leading Russia's “Equator Task Force” is helping Venezuela train its army, which comes as Putin pledged to support its ally in case of a conflict - although the logistics of real help would be very, very tough for them now. I’ll mention again that Maduro urged Trump to avoid a “forever war” in the region (similar to the one in Afghanistan) shortly after the situation started, while Trump is now blaming Venezuela for “dumping hundreds of thousands of people” from prisons into the US.

So, you can see how this has the potential to degenerate into something that can lead to what I believe could be even unprecedented disruptions in the oil market, as mentioned before. This comes even though Venezuela is far from the biggest producer and would probably be subdued relatively easily, but it’s more of a matter of what this country actually is on a global scale - one of the founding members of OPEC and the state with the largest proven oil reserves on the planet, with nearly twice the reserves of the US and Russia combined.

In the longer-term however, depending on how this plays out, we could even see a lot more production entering the global market (in case the US takes over and depletes the country’s reserves faster), which can actually be bad for the price of oil, while a war in the region can easily degenerate into disruptions for many other commodities in the near-term, which combined can lead to a new wave of inflation in the US, while entering a new war so close to home can weaken the country’s image, currency, and even ratings.

OPEC and its allies also agreed to boost their oil production in December but will halt these increases in Q1 2026 at least, although a potential attack on Venezuela can change this; keep it in mind. This has the potential to boost oil prices, as OPEC’s previous hikes this year were a massive factor behind this year’s crash.

This is combined with reports of rising US crude inventories, although OPEC even came back and said that certain international media outlets “distorted the data” presented in their Monthly Oil Market Report. Still, the bearish sentiment continued and was reinforced by projections such as JPMorgan’s that talk about a potentially “overwhelming market oversupply,” potentially making oil prices plummet to $30 per barrel by 2027.

Again, this remains uncertain, and it should serve as more of a “what if” scenario than a concrete reality, and we’ve seen the same J.P. Morgan saying that oil could go to $100 in 2024 back when it was trading at around $70 to $80.

The war in Ukraine also took some steps towards a potential resolution, with Ukraine agreeing to most of the draft proposed by the US (with some “minor” details to settle) but also being pressured into taking action fast. The EU also proposed some revisions together with Ukraine, but keep in mind that this is likely not the end, and long-term peace even after another agreement is questionable, which is why the EU and Ukraine aren’t that hasty in accepting this, but it’s a step in that direction.

However, I’ll mention again that when it comes to a majority of commodities (especially oil and gas due to Russia’s production), remember that this war and expectations of it prior to the actual attack were massive factors in oil’s jump from ~$65 in December 2021 to nearly $120 in March 2022. This leads to the risk of seeing a reversal over time that can add further pressure on oil prices, and the market will definitely pay very close attention to it. Although I don’t expect Russia to go back to its previous levels in global trade in the foreseeable future, this can still come with more pressure on the price of oil.

We’ve also seen Trump’s sanctions on Russian oil significantly hurting companies like Rosneft and Lukoil (Russia’s key oil companies), with the latter selling their international operations, prompting the US to conclude that the sanctions hit as intended. It makes sense that it took so long to see this from an “oil stability” point of view, as there was fear that this move would’ve caused oil to jump above $150 per barrel back then (from ~$120 when the war began) and potentially help Putin by making the remaining oil more valuable, while the market is now more stable and adapted, but it’s also kind of disingenuous given the stated support for Ukraine, in my opinion.

I’ll also mention that TotalEnergies (TTE) released their annual energy outlook report recently, with the big difference being that they are now estimating a new peak in oil demand that’s an entire decade further into the future than last year’s expectations. This is the result of the slower/shifted green ambitions seen from a lot of oil companies, as they’re returning to their roots of oil and gas operations due in part to political and economic pressure.

As they say, this is a result of a mix between Trump’s policies around oil and gas, LNG licensing resumptions and green rollbacks, alongside coal plant installations in Asia and a slower global EV adoption.

Risks Reiterated

So, we’re talking about a ton of uncertainty, at least in the near-term given the macro factors, making it risky or at least very likely to be volatile at the end of the day. A drop to or below $40 per barrel, for example, can cause significant issues for Chord, as even with the recent Williston Basin acquisition and cost savings, the company may risk not breaking even despite the long laterals saving a lot.

Their balance sheet should allow them to survive for some years, but no US oil company is ultimately “completely” safe, and there’s certainly potential to see this trade much lower before it gets any better.

Updated Valuation

Once again, despite all the savings and efficiencies, I’ll continue to use a lower FCF on purpose, just to emphasize how unbelievably cheap CHRD is right now. Starting with $700 million in FCF in 2025 to account for even lower oil prices and a good margin of safety, I’ll assume that they maintain this FCF in 2026 and 2027 as they're developing. Again, I'll assume that the company manages to reduce its CAPEX to $1.1 billion by 2028, while also growing their efficiency and production during this time to reach $1.2 billion in free cash flow in 2028 (I’m lowballing on purpose).

I'll also assume that they only grow at 3.5% for the subsequent 2 years and 2.5% for the other 5 after that, with a very low 1.75% terminal growth rate and a WACC of 9% (well above my estimated 7.33% based on a cost of equity of ~7.7%, cost of debt of 6.7%) for an even better margin of safety, using their $849.62 million in net debt and 57.16 million shares outstanding as of the Q3 report.

This way, we get to an enterprise value of $15.3 billion, an equity value of $14.46 billion, and an intrinsic value of $252.89 per share, which is a massive increase compared to the current price even when using conservative estimates in everything.

Conclusions

Chord Energy remains one of the best opportunities in US oil right now, trading at a deep discount to fair value even in a conservative scenario, with significant upside potential and very solid financial health. Even if the stock won’t get to this estimated fair value, the company should be able to return massive amounts of money back to its shareholders, while maintaining a healthy balance sheet and coming at a very solid risk-reward, in my opinion.

data.title: Advantage Energy: Record Well Drilled
data.description:
Advantage Energy (AAVVF) announced that a record-size well was drilled and the follow-up well was nearly as good. The first well came in at 4,567 BOED and produced some liquids. The next well came in at 3,067 BOED while producing only natural gas. That first well is about quintuple the production that was achieved routinely when I first began coverage of this company on the website some years back. It is enough of an advance that it may shift the company emphasis back towards natural gas in the future (which would slow the transition to liquids). That has been an ongoing situation for the company ever since I have covered the advances in technology that have steadily lowered costs.

One consequence of more productive wells is that the capital budget for fiscal year 2026 is about C$10 million lower than the original 3-year plan due to the advance. Obviously less money is needed for production goals if the production of each well advances. This company has been announcing record sized wells every few years ever since I began coverage.

The announcement could not have come at a better time because natural gas prices appear to be strengthening. That is very likely to mean that increasing profitability is going to be met with rising natural gas prices to give this company an extra boost over much of the industry.

Relative Acreage Profitability

The shift in profitability from the technology advances combined with strengthening natural gas prices and weak oil prices can be seen below:

(Note: Advantage Energy is a Canadian producer that reports using Canadian dollars unless otherwise noted.)

What has shifted is that the natural gas prices may well be better than the relationship shown above. It was not that long ago that I was writing about the natural gas prices being worse than the relationship shown above.

But the key here is that the natural gas acreage is going to be more profitable (possibly even more profitable than shown above depending upon how the future unfolds).

The last article noted that the polar vortex event could strengthen natural gas prices going forward if enough natural gas in storage was used. We are now about midway through that event, and it seems to be meeting the initial promises of a major cold event that would last a while. But that makes the shift in potential profitability more important.

It is not that long ago that I was reporting on AECO going negative for a brief time in the third quarter. This management responded by shutting in production while purchasing natural gas for negative prices. But this event meant that the liquids acreage was more than "pulling its weight" at keeping the company near breakeven when lots of competition were reporting losses.

2026 Budget Guidance

Now it looks as though the natural gas production will gain importance, at least temporarily. For the fiscal year 2026 budget guidance, there are more than a few natural gas wells that will be drilled first. This is to take advantage of the relatively strong winter natural gas prices combined with a polar vortex event.

However, there are some Charlie Lake interval wells on the schedule because flexibility is important in an industry where conditions can change rapidly.

Despite the fact that the major gas processing plant is going to be down for about 3 weeks for a turnaround (whose effects will last several years), there is still a projection to grow production for the fiscal year.

What will happen in fiscal year 2027 is that average production will go up simply because the plant turnaround is absent. Any growth in average production will be in addition to that absence.

For this year, the second quarter comparison will be especially weak due to the plant turnaround because this company only has one major natural gas processing plant. So, when a turnaround does happen, much of the production is shut-in until the turnaround completes.

New Gas Plant

There is a second, smaller plant that will be commissioned down where the liquids are to take some pressure off the larger plant. Currently, plans call for both plants to be operating after the second quarter.

These smaller plants, like the one to be commissioned in the second quarter (shown above), are far less significant than the one on the legacy acreage where this company began operations. But they also service proportionately more liquids and other important functions (like water disposal).

If enough of these eventually come into being, then the periodic turnarounds of the company's main processing plant on the gassy acreage will decline in importance in the future.

Summary

What is important about more well production is that the new technology either maintain the current cost structure or, hopefully, improve that cost structure.

Management has so far kept the well payouts in excellent territory, as shown above. That strongly implies that costs are being monitored as technology moves forward.

This likely means very good things for a natural gas producer during a time of strengthening AECO prices.

What is even more important is the flexibility gained with some liquids production for the company to survive or even thrive under a wider variety of industry conditions. This company has long shifted drilling to wells that are profitable under the current industry conditions. Investors can expect that agility to continue well into the future.

Advantage Energy remains a strong buy consideration even though risk is elevated due to the debt levels and the relatively small size of the company. Cost control is excellent, and the company keeps growing production successfully.

This is a small company that should be part of a basket of smaller companies while being allowed to grow to a point where the market is a bit more efficient. I would therefore continue to hang on to these shares unless the story changes or the prices include several good years of future earnings.

Risks

Commodity prices are both volatile and low visibility. A severe and sustained downturn can materially change the outlook of the company.

There is some political uncertainty marked by threats of trade wars and tariffs that needs to be replaced with something permanent and constructive. There is also the issue that the United States may be headed for a recession or potentially worse. Anything like this could materially affect the company's profitability if it lasts.

There is some risk to the elevated debt level until that debt gets paid down or until the convertible debt converts. Such companies can fail even with the best management because debt needs to be serviced regardless of industry conditions. Therefore, the finances may limit some of the company's flexibility despite the presence of significant liquids production.

On the other hand, some of the new intervals coming online in Canada have extremely low breakeven costs. These new intervals may enable company survival even under some very hostile industry conditions.

Disclaimer: I am not an investment advisor, and this article is not meant to be a recommendation for the purchase or sale of stock. Investors are advised to review all company documents and press releases to see if the company fits its own investment qualifications.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Gold And Silver: Don't Forget About Miners
data.description:
Mining ETFs have outpaced gold and silver performance

Investors with exposure to precious metals, in particular gold and silver, are having a good time. Although as gold and silver prices keep marching towards new highs and outpacing other assets by wide margins (see the chart below), two logical questions come to mind. First, how much more appreciation potential is left? And second, are there other alternative ideas? The goal of this article is to provide our thoughts on both questions. Our short answer to the first question is that we are still seeing a good amount of upside potential for both silver and gold despite their YTD advances. For the second question, we want to draw your attention to gold and silver mining stocks such as those held in the VanEck Junior Gold Miners ETF (GDXJ) and/or in the VanEck Gold Miners ETF (GDX)

To better contextualize the rest of the discussion, let's examine the chart below more carefully. This chart illustrates the YTD performance of various investment assets. As seen, silver (as approximated by the ABRDN Physical Silver Shares ETF (SIVR)) delivered a triple-digit return of 119%, and gold (approximated by the SPDR Gold Shares (GLD)) increased 62%, both far outpacing other assets such as the SP500, bonds, and cryptos (as approximated by the iShares Bitcoin Trust (IBIT)). As impressive as the return from gold and silver is thus far, they are not the strongest performers. Miner ETFs, both GDXJ and GDX, saw far better price change YTD, with GDXJ rising 167% and GDX rising 153%.

With this backdrop, next, I will explain why I keep seeing favorable fundamentals that can catalyze continued outperformance for gold, silver, and especially mining stocks.

Miners have passed their inflecting point

Besides the above price changes, a key fundamental trigger to this article is the FQ3 earnings reports (ER) released by major mining companies. As two examples, the two charts below show the recent earnings history for AEM and NEM, two leading and bellwether companies in the mining sector. Both showed continued year-over-year (YoY) revenue growth in FQ3 as seen. To wit, AEM's revenue grew $3.06 B in the Sep quarter, translating into an annual growth rate of 41.93%, the highest YoY growth in the past 12 months. Similarly, NEM’s revenue reached $5.52 B in FQ3 2025, representing a 19.96% annual increase and a continuation of double-digit YOY growth in recent quarters.

At this point, a bit of background could be helpful for readers unfamiliar with mining stocks. This article uses the term “miners” in the title to be intentionally ambiguous as I see no need or feasibility to differentiate gold miners from silver miners. As detailed in a recent article (titled “Gold And Silver: The Relevance Of Copper”),

When a miner mine one metal, it inevitably produces the other in significant amount as a byproduct. More often than not, the amount is significant enough to make it difficult to tell which metal is the by product.

Despite such ambiguity, the underlying fundamentals for miners, regardless of their focus (gold, silver, or even copper), are largely governed by the same dynamics. Among the many forces, a key mechanism on my list is the lead time for miners to expand or develop new capacities relative to the changes in commodity prices. The lead time could be as long as years (see this report for an example). The second mechanism that can impact miners’ profits sensitively is operating expenses, which could increase – and have increased in the past often - faster than gold and silver prices. The earnings of miners do not scale with the prices of their end products but with the gap between prices and operating expenses.

As a case in point, both NEM and AEM have suffered large surges in their operating expenses during the year between mid-2021 and mid-2023, as highlighted in the red box in the chart below. The YOY increases in their operation expenses were on average ~50% during that time. Such a large increase in cost could more than offset the gain from higher commodities prices during the same period, and the net results could worsen – not better – profits despite more favorable pricing environments.

Judging by the recent ERs from leading miners (like those presented earlier), I believe the headwinds of both mechanisms have run their course by now. As such, I expect continued profit momentum ahead for miners, as detailed next.

Miners' profit momentum likely to continue

Out of the many signs that can support my above assessment, I will limit myself to the top 2 to stay on point: the CAPEX expenditures from miners and their margins. After reading the Q3 ER for several miners, we’ve observed a stabilization or decrease in their CAPEX expenditures. The chart below illustrates NEM and AEM’s CAPEX in Q3 as two data points. As seen, NEM’s CAPEX peaked at the end of 2023 at around $780 M. Since then, its CAPEX has been on an overall downward trend, with a reading of $727 M for FQ3. AEM’s CAPEX has shown moderate increases in absolute dollar amount and reached $632 M in FQ3. However, the pace of increase was far milder than those I’ve seen in earlier years, and also the increase was more than offset by the robust earnings growth. As an example, the second chart below shows that its CAPEX a fraction of its sales has been on an overall downward trend since 2023.

More importantly, I believe miners’ CAPEX expenditures in recent years have finally overcome the lead time as aforementioned. Now, they begin to bring about a profitability boost, especially when combined with the favorable gold and silver prices we are experiencing now. As a reflection of their emphasis on profitability, the following chart shows their gross profit margins (GPM) in recent years. As of Q3, NEM’s GPM is reported to be 48.3%. It is not only far better than its 5-year average GPM of 36.5% but also represents the peak level in at least 5 years. The picture for AEM is very similar.

Other risks and final thoughts

As another positive for precious metals (and also a key reason why both silver and gold are my long-term core holdings), gold, silver, and also mining stocks have demonstrated relatively low correlation with respect to other asset classes. I feel such excellent diversification power is of particular relevance under current market conditions where equity valuation is quite elevated.

Another upside risk worth mentioning is the latest interest rate cut announced by the Federal Reserve on Wednesday. Historical data has shown a close correlation between the movements of gold prices and real interest rates (as an example, see the long-term data tracked by PIMCO as shown in second chart below). With everything else being equal, a lower notional rate (which is what the Fed controls) makes the real interest rate lower, and thus can support higher gold prices (and also silver prices too, although the correlation for silver is less tight than for gold).

In terms of downside risks, the negatives generic to gold and silver have been thoroughly analyzed by many other authors. So here, I will limit the discussion to the negatives that are directly relevant to the analyzes presented above. To start, mining stocks – with surprise at all - are strongly correlated with gold and silver prices. As such, including them in your portfolio doesn't provide too much additional diversification if you already hold gold and/or silver. For instance, if we look at the correlation matrix above again, we can see GDX’s correlation with silver is 0.72 and with gold is even stronger at 0.83.

Second, mining stocks tend to amplify the price movements of gold and silver. As aforementioned, miners’ profitability scales with the difference between spot metal prices and their operating expenses, which makes mining stocks a leverage play on gold and silver prices in my view. As a reflection, even for ETFs such as GDX and GDXJ, their price volatilities are more elevated than both traditional assets (such as SP500 and/or bonds) and also silver and gold prices themselves. Let’s take a final look at the correlation matrix above, and we will see that GDX has exhibited an average daily standard deviation of 2.59% in the past. This is about 2.5x of gold’s 1.09% standard deviation and about 2x of SPY’s 1.26% daily deviation.

To close, the goal of this article is to share my view on two particular questions: A) How much more price appreciation potential is left for gold and silver, and B) Are there alternative ideas? I see these questions, or similar ones, more frequently these days given the large rallies in gold and silver prices. For the reasons presented above, my answer is that there is still plenty of upside potential ahead, and mining stocks are particularly interesting under current conditions.

data.title: Qualcomm: The Most Underappreciated EPS Upside Story In Semis
data.description:
I view Qualcomm (QCOM) as a quality stock that is looking for its next leg of the rally. The current optionality around AI-driven handset ASP uplift and credible automotive revenue conversion appear to be two monetizable levers that could lift EPS structurally and earn a re-rating after a muted range in the past 5 years. EPS has not broken out beyond the $12 mark.

Valuations did test higher multiples (in price to forward earnings) in the early phase of the 2024 bull markets, at a time when EPS was on an upward trajectory. The upward EPS trends are still intact, but valuations have retraced to the 5-year average levels. Given the EPS expectations from FY 2026 is almost flat, my thesis finds a bullish stance in sustained earnings lift from the AI and automotive optionality. If that happens, the stock would benefit from the double effects of a better than expected EPS trajectory and a sustained lift leading to rerating as well.

Premium Android and Automotive

The most important growth lever in my view is structural expansion already in progress in the premium Android tier. QCT handset revenues grew 14% YoY in Q4 FY2025 even in what management noted as a flat handset market. This implies that Qualcomm’s revenue per device is now rising regardless of whether global smartphone units grow. This is because consumers in developed as well as emerging markets are now gravitating to premium devices. This revenue per unit support is expected to be a multi-year support now.

... important to keep in mind as well is this very significant mix shift towards more premium devices. And that's not about content growth within the tier, but it's more about more capable devices being purchased by consumer. And that is a multiyear trend as well that we're continuing to see going forward

The growth in premium device demand also means higher compute requirements, i.e., higher chipset spending per device. Qualcomm’s share of the premium tier, especially Samsung's demand, has also structurally shifted higher (75% cited as the new baseline).

The other core lever, QCT Automotive, saw 17% YoY growth in Q4 FY2025 and demand is in an accelerating phase. More importantly, Qualcomm is moving beyond connectivity into higher-value ADAS with the commercial debut of Snapdragon Ride Pilot. The automotive segment’s 36% growth in FY25, now at a $4b+ annual run rate, gives Qualcomm a reliable second engine with multi-year visibility. This diversification reduces earnings cyclicality and provides incremental margin leverage as high-value ADAS content ramps.

Additional EPS Levers

Beyond handsets and automotive, Qualcomm is adding multiple growth contributors that already showed up in Q4. IoT revenues grew 7% YoY, driven by industrial, networking, and fixed-wireless products. XR demand is rising sharply, with management stating they are significantly ahead of prior expectations as Meta, Samsung, and others scale smart-glasses designs. These categories broaden QCT’s base and reduce dependence on handset cycles.

AI PCs represent another visible driver, supported by Qualcomm’s superior performance and around 150 design wins expected through 2026. This gives Qualcomm significant exposure to the first wave of on-device AI adoption in laptops. In parallel, Qualcomm is entering cloud AI inference with its AI200/AI250 platforms, with revenue pulled forward to fiscal 2027 and described as a multi-billion dollar opportunity.

The Buy Thesis

To quantify the potential EPS uplift in FY2026, I created an EPS model using conservative estimates across the board and assuming the most visible and monetizable levers already starting to show up in FY2025. This alone shows an immediate uplift of ~8.5% in EPS without the need for multi-year projections and assumptions. It also shows that Qualcomm is poised to break through the $12 EPS ceiling that has often been a resistance in the past 5 years.

The biggest positive accretion comes from the premium handset trends, accounting for almost half the EPS uplift, even when I have assumed a far more conservative 5% growth in QCT revenues in FY2026 (compared to the 14% growth reported in Q4). Automotive then adds another layer of predictable earnings - here too I have modeled a conservative 20% growth versus the Q4 36% growth reported. IoT and XR growth in Q4 was ~22%, but I have dialed it down to 10%. This conservatism across growth assumptions is somewhere in the middle - from numbers observed in Q4 to management guidance on continuing momentum for the next fiscal year -

But when you look at our strong business momentum exiting fiscal '25, you see the benefit of that showed up in our results also showing up in the December quarter guidance. And so that carries forward into the rest of the fiscal year.

An ~8.5% EPS uplift with conservative growth extrapolation in the near term implies a far higher floor for the share prices by itself - particularly because the expectations are flat. I see a high probability of this earnings beat translating into results over the next few quarters. And that should mean rerating potential for the stock, which has now stagnated (relatively speaking) for the past few years. Even a 10-20% rerating push could mean a PE ratio of ~16.5x-18x - something that cannot be ruled out if the EPS growth story keeps up as I expect, and a structural floor in terms of handset cyclicality is sustained. Importantly, my uplift analysis excludes all FY2027 drivers like data-center AI ramp, additional ADAS scaling, and the broader AI PC cycle. These remain on the table.

A yield of ~2% is also a meaningful addition to the Qualcomm bull thesis because the downside is limited. Dividends not only have a 22-year growth streak (inflation beating dividend growth), but also look extremely protected by a conservative payout ratio of below 30%.

Overall, Qualcomm appears to be in a spot where the downside is restricted. It has also not moved up as much as several other semiconductor stocks (though other stocks like AMD or Nvidia had greater AI exposure too). As markets search for the next breakout story in semis, investors could see money rotating into a fairly valued stock like Qualcomm with room to grow. The growth expectations may not be explosive - my analysis points to 10-30% upside over the next year - but the risk-reward asymmetry is favorable. Dividends add to Qualcomm's Buy thesis.

data.title: Autonomous Vehicles: Accelerating The Future Of Mobility
data.description:
By Daniel Spelman & Christopher Vandergrift, CFA

Autonomous vehicles (AVs) are beginning to deliver on their commercial promise after more than a decade of development. Driverless fleets from Waymo (WAYMO), Baidu’s Apollo Go (BIDU), Pony.ai (PONY), WeRide (WRD) and Zoox (ZOOX) now operate across more than 20 cities worldwide. In the United States, Waymo’s 1,500-vehicle fleet accounts for roughly 0.3% of ride-hailing miles. While still early, this validates that autonomous mobility works at a meaningful scale.

Several forces are converging to accelerate AV adoption: rapid gains in artificial intelligence (AI) compute power, the maturation of sensor and safety systems, and a more supportive regulatory environment. AVs have moved from a research challenge to a reality and appear poised to enter a broader commercial phase as more providers scale deployments. In our view, the next five to seven years could bring one of the most significant transformations in urban mobility in decades as autonomous fleets expand and become more widely accessible.

A key implication for investors is that autonomy is likely to meaningfully expand the total addressable market for on-demand transportation. As AV technology lowers the cost of supply and broadens availability, on-demand mobility should capture a larger share of daily travel, unlocking growth opportunities across hardware, software, original equipment manufacturers (OEMs), platforms and fleet operators.

A broadening AV ecosystem

Advances in AI compute are enabling a more diverse set of AV participants. Nvidia’s (NVDA) new reference architecture for Level 4 autonomy is designed as a foundational platform for self-driving software, providing standardized compute, sensor configurations and safety systems that OEMs can adopt without building their own autonomous stack from scratch. Stellantis (STLA), Mercedes-Benz (MBGAF), GM (GM), Volvo (VOLAF) and Lucid (LCID) are integrating this architecture into future vehicles, with production expected as early as 2028. Uber (UBER) has also indicated plans to deploy up to 100,000 AVs through OEM and AV software partnerships.

This points to a structure similar to the early smartphone market. Nvidia’s platform could become an Android-like layer used broadly across OEMs in addition to providing the chips for AV software providers such as Waymo, Apollo Go, Pony.ai and WeRide to build differentiated systems on top. Value is likely to be created at multiple levels of this ecosystem, including compute, software, sensors, manufacturing and mobility platforms. Together, these developments establish a foundation for broad AV deployment over the next decade.

The exhibit below highlights the range of levels across AV models — from driver assistance to full automation.

Removing the human driver also reshapes the economics of mobility. Lower operating costs, higher utilization and greater availability are likely to increase usage, reduce reliance on second-car ownership and attract demand from public transit routes. Rather than shifting share from one type of driver to another, autonomy should expand the overall demand for on-demand transportation and set the stage for novel business models.

Technology differentiation and operational benefits

AVs offer several operational advantages beyond labor and vehicle cost savings. Electric drivetrains reduce maintenance and fuel costs. Predictive-maintenance capabilities improve reliability and help keep fleets on the road longer.

Tesla: A potential threat to ride-hailing players

Tesla (TSLA) represents a unique approach within the AV landscape. As a vertically integrated, low-cost, and AI- and camera-only solution, Tesla’s success in this market would present a credible threat to incumbent ride-hailing players. This architecture remains in development and has faced safety-related scrutiny, but it has the potential to support a lower-cost AV platform if validated at scale. Tesla also benefits from a large and continuously growing real-world dataset that could accelerate learning cycles and contribute to faster deployment if the system reaches required safety thresholds.

The company plans to begin production of its Cybercab, a robotaxi-specific vehicle, starting in the second quarter of 2026. By mid-2027, the Hardware 5 chips should help support more advanced robotaxi operations, and looking much further out, Tesla’s Optimus humanoid robots could provide cost advantages around cleaning and maintenance.

Safety remains a critical dimension of AV adoption. Human error accounts for most U.S. traffic accidents. Early data from commercial AV fleets show meaningful reductions in critical incidents relative to human driving. Improved safety performance could influence insurance costs and build public trust as deployments expand. Many U.S. states allow full deployment and testing of autonomous driving.

Contrasting industry architectures

If multiple AV providers achieve commercialization concurrently, the industry is more likely to evolve toward a modular structure in which specialized firms focus on software development, compute, manufacturing, fleet operations and marketplace aggregation. Mobility platforms such as Uber and Lyft (LYFT) should continue to play an important role as demand aggregators and coordination layers, particularly as AV supply becomes more fragmented across OEMs and software providers.

Tesla is pursuing a more vertically integrated model. Its strategy includes proprietary AI training infrastructure, custom chips, vehicle manufacturing and early efforts to build a first-party robotaxi network. If proven, this approach could resemble an Apple-like integrated ecosystem in which hardware, software and services are tightly linked. In parallel, a broader Android-like ecosystem may develop around Nvidia’s architecture, OEM production and AV software from Waymo, Pony.ai, Apollo Go and others. Both approaches could succeed and offer different paths to value creation.

The bottom line

Autonomous technology is likely to reshape mobility over the coming decade. Lower costs, improved safety and new service models have the potential to dramatically influence commuting patterns, urban design and personal vehicle ownership, as well as impact adjacent industries such as insurance.

For investors, the central insight is that AVs should expand the total demand for on-demand transportation and support the potential for multiple long-term winners across the ecosystem as autonomous fleets scale globally.
