data.title: Oracle's Plunge Is A Gift
data.description:
The last few months have been really difficult for shareholders of technology behemoth Oracle (ORCL). In September, the stock skyrocketed after management unveiled a massive surge in backlog. The expectation at that time was that revenue, and therefore profits and cash flows, would grow massively in the coming years. But since my article about that jump, the stock has plummeted 39.7%. This is at a time when the S&P 500 is up 5.3%. A big chunk of this decline came on December 11th after management announced financial results for the second quarter of the company's 2026 fiscal year.

I'm less concerned about how the company performed relative to what analysts were anticipating. Although some discussion of that is warranted. What's more intriguing to me is that, by pretty much every measure, the company is doing a stellar job. In fact, back in October, management even increased their expectations for cloud infrastructure revenue for the next several years.

Naturally, there are always risks here. Over-investment in the AI space could prove to be a problem if it results in decreased spending in the future. And right now, even after the tumble, the stock is rather expensive. But the way I view it, achieving even some of its projected growth can lead to massive upside. And it is because of that expectation that I believe that maintaining it as a 'strong buy' candidate is the right choice.

The market is pricing in a catastrophe

On December 11th, shares of Oracle closed down 10.8%. This move lower came after management announced financial results for the second quarter of the company's 2026 fiscal year. Revenue amounted to $16.06 billion. This was up 14.2% over the $14.06 billion the company reported a year earlier. Normally, such a year-over-year increase would create a lot of optimism amongst investors. But shares of the company fell in part because revenue missed analysts' expectations by $134.2 million.

The growth that the company saw was really disjointed. What I mean by this is that it was fueled almost entirely by its Cloud and Software business. This is the largest part of the company, providing cloud oriented applications and infrastructure technologies to customers who need them. It also offers up certain types of software and software support services. During the second quarter, revenue for this segment amounted to $13.85 billion. This was 15.4% above the $12 billion the company reported a year earlier.

Such a strong increase is great to see. But even within that unit, the growth was heavily tilted toward two particular activities. Cloud applications revenue, as an example, jumped from $3.50 billion to $3.90 billion. Even more impressive was cloud infrastructure. This revenue skyrocketed from $2.43 billion to $4.08 billion. According to management, existing customers renewed their cloud contracts, and there was more robust demand from new customers. This makes sense to me. The fact of the matter is that the cloud computing market is already massive, and it is slated to grow significantly over the next several years.

Back in September of last year, investment bank Goldman Sachs (GS) estimated that the cloud computing market was worth about $496 billion in 2023. It projected that this industry would grow to $2 trillion by the end of this decade. Other estimates vary of course. One source, as an example, estimated that the market was worth about $595.7 billion back in 2024. And by the end of 2027, it expects it to grow $1 trillion or greater. And another source expects the market opportunity to expand from $805 billion last year to approximately double that by 2028. Truthfully, nobody knows what the future holds. But one thing that we can say with a high degree of certainty is that we will see stupendous growth in this space for the foreseeable future. And as a big player in this market, though not as big as some of its competitors, it definitely stands to benefit.

By comparison, other parts of the company were not as impressive. Even within the Cloud and Software segment, there was some weakness. Overall software license revenue dropped from $1.20 billion to $939 million. And even though it grew, the software support side of the business increased only slightly from $4.87 billion to $4.94 billion. Other operating segments saw growth as well. As an example, the Hardware segment reported a rise in revenue from $728 million to $776 million. Management attributed this to some of its strategic hardware product offerings and attractive demand for its Oracle Exadata technology. And then finally, the Services segment reported an increase in revenue from $1.33 billion to $1.43 billion. This was because of greater demand for its consulting services, according to management.

On the bottom line, things were even better. Earnings per share nearly doubled from $1.10 to $2.10. In fact, the earnings per share reported by the company ended up being a whopping $0.93 per share above what analysts were hoping to see. It is important to keep in mind, however, that a lot of this improvement was driven by a $2.7 billion pre-tax gain that the company booked from its sale of Ampere Computing Holdings. This was ultimately sold off for $4.3 billion after management decided that it was no longer core to its plans. Specifically, the company said that they sold Ampere, 'because we no longer think it is strategic for us to continue designing, manufacturing, and using our own chips in our cloud data centers.' The company has decided to be chip neutral and to essentially work with all CPU and GPU suppliers. But of course, it did also emphasize its plans to continue buying the latest GPUs from NVIDIA (NVDA), which over the last several years now has become the undisputed leader in the market.

The rise in earnings per share took net profits up from $3.15 billion to $6.14 billion. Other profitability metrics performed well also. Operating cash flow jumped from $1.30 billion to $2.07 billion. If we strip out changes in working capital, we would get a rise from $5.54 billion to $6.83 billion. And finally, EBITDA for the company expanded from $5.64 billion to $7.42 billion. In the chart above, you can also see financial results for the first half of the 2026 fiscal year compared to the same time in 2025. What this shows is that the company is doing a fantastic job. But of course, as I mentioned at the start of this article, the market is taking a rather bearish stance on the company right now.

If we take the results that we have seen from the company so far this year, we would expect adjusted operating cash flow to come in at around $27.34 billion. Meanwhile, we would be looking at EBITDA of around $28.06 billion. Using these estimates, and also using the results that we saw for the company in 2024, we can see in the chart above how shares of the company are currently priced. These are very high multiples and would typically elicit either a 'hold' rating or a 'sell' rating out of me. But there are some reasons why I am very bullish even with how expensive the stock is. For starters, compared to other similar enterprises, shares are quite cheap. In the table below, I compared it to five similar companies. What I found was that, on a price-to-operating-cash-flow basis, Oracle was the cheapest of the group. And only one of these companies is cheaper than it is on an EV to EBITDA basis.

Company Price/Operating Cash Flow EV/EBITDA
Oracle 20.7 23.4
Microsoft (MSFT) 24.6 20.3
ServiceNow (NOW) 37.5 74.1
Palo Alto Networks (PANW) 33.9 66.1
CrowdStrike (CRWD) 88.3 63,186.6
Fortinet (FTNT) 26.0 27.0

This alone would not typically be enough to justify a bullish outlook on my end. But there are other reasons as well. For starters, management announced another jump in backlog during the quarter. Total backlog came in at $523 billion. That's up massively from the $97 billion the company reported a year earlier. It also represents a gain of $68 billion compared to what the company had during the first quarter of 2026. This is a monumental amount of upside for what is essentially future revenue.

What's really exciting here is that, back in October of this year, management made a revision to their expectations regarding cloud revenue over the next several years. This year, the company should generate around $10 billion from those activities. Next year, we are looking at about $18 billion. The previous forecast by management called for overall revenue here to hit $144 billion by 2030. But now, that curve higher is steeper. They now anticipate $166 billion in annual revenue by 2030. In between 2026 and 2030, the increase in expected revenue here amounts to an extra $43 billion.

In my previous article, I used management's expectations, as well as reasonable assumptions, to project out what the future for the company would look like from a revenue, profit, and cash flow perspective. In light of the changes that we have seen, incorporating not only this new guidance from management, but also factoring in other reasonable growth for other parts of the business, we can see in the tables below how much in revenue and profits the company should generate each year between 2026 and 2030. Needless to say, this is a remarkable amount of growth for such a large business.

What we have here is a company that should go from net profits of around $17.4 billion next year to profits of $111.3 billion by the end of the decade. Cash flows should scale up similarly. And this assumes that management does not capture any meaningful margin improvements. In all likelihood, they will.

In the table above, you can see what this would mean for the overall valuation of the company from a trading multiple standpoint. Shares quickly go from looking drastically overpriced compared to the companies outside of this space to looking like deep value plays if current pricing holds. By the end of the decade, we are looking at low single digit multiples in some respects. But the big question now is how we project out what kind of upside potential might exist because of this.

In my last article, the method that I used was to assume that shares of Oracle would continue to trade at the same multiples that they are trading at today. I also took a second scenario where I compared it to the cheapest of the five competitors I stacked it up against and did the same type of analysis. In that case, and in this case now, that would be Microsoft. In the chart above, you can see the annualized upside implied from this assessment. We are looking at an upside of between 56.8% and 63.1% on an annual basis. Even if the company achieves half of that, it would mean significant outperformance compared to the market.

Takeaway

The way I view it, the picture for shareholders of Oracle is looking very positive right now. I know that the market has moved against the stock. And honestly, the one risk factor here is that we might be in an AI bubble. This is something that I have written about before. But considering how cheap the company is and the fact that we should continue to see growth in data centers even if AI expansion is slower than expected, shares don't look like they would be a bad opportunity at this point. In light of all of this, I believe that maintaining it as a 'strong buy' candidate is the right move.

data.title: AI Infrastructure: Buy The Buildings, Not The GPUs
data.description:
The AI infrastructure build-out represents a trillion-dollar opportunity over the next decade. Power scarcity, not computing power, is proving to be the scarcest resource. Against this backdrop, pure-play digital landlords who provide powered AI-ready facilities, companies like Applied Digital (APLD), Cipher Mining (CIFR), Core Scientific (CORZ), Galaxy Digital (GLXY), and TeraWulf (WULF) offer superior risk-adjusted returns compared to neocloud operators competing in the compute layer. This isn't an argument that neoclouds like IREN Limited (IREN), Nebius Group (NBIS), and CoreWeave (CRWV) won't generate returns; they likely will. Rather, it's a recognition that leasing scarce powered facilities is a lower-risk business model than operating GPU clusters and competing for compute customers.

The investment case rests on three key facts:

  1. Power delivery timelines create structural scarcity. Large data center projects can take 3-5 years just to energize facilities due to utility interconnection queues and associated infrastructure construction. Companies that control sites with power already delivered or have shortened timelines to energization are disproportionately valuable in a market where hyperscalers can't simply write checks to get gigawatts delivered next quarter.
  2. The facilities layer avoids compute-layer risks. Neoclouds face several challenges, which include fast chip depreciation, intense hyperscaler competition, and margin compression as AI workloads standardize. Digital landlords simply lease powered space, avoiding the technology obsolescence and competitive pressures inherent to operating GPU clusters and selling compute services.
  3. Asset longevity favors real estate over servers. Facilities infrastructure operates for 30-50 years, while GPU clusters depreciate in 2-3 years as new chip generations emerge. This buildings-versus-GPUs durability mismatch fundamentally alters financing costs and long-term risk profiles.

Understanding the AI Infrastructure Stack

Before diving into why pure-play facilities offer superior returns, it's helpful to break down the AI infrastructure landscape into four distinct layers or building blocks:

Power: Companies that generate electricity, such as nuclear plants, natural gas facilities, renewables, and emerging technologies like small modular reactors. These sit upstream of everything and represent the ultimate supply constraint.

Facilities: Companies that deliver power to data centers and provide the physical buildings, cooling systems, and electrical distribution. This is where pure facilities players like Applied Digital, Cipher Mining, Core Scientific, Galaxy Digital, and TeraWulf operate. They control powered capacity and lease it to tenants. This layer includes all the infrastructure to get power from generators to racks. Many of the companies that operate in this space are former Bitcoin miners who were fortuitously positioned for the AI boom. To survive, many became experts in securing, operating, and scaling power infrastructure in challenging environments.

Chips: The semiconductor layer includes GPUs (Nvidia (NVDA), AMD (AMD)), custom AI accelerators (Google's (GOOG) (GOOGL) TPUs, Amazon's (AMZN) Trainium), networking equipment, and memory systems. This layer faces rapid innovation cycles and depreciation.

Compute: Companies that operate GPU clusters and sell compute as a service. This includes hyperscalers like Amazon and Microsoft (MSFT), but also so-called neocloud providers like IREN Limited, Nebius, and CoreWeave. This layer manages workloads, provides APIs, and interfaces directly with end customers.

This article focuses specifically on the facilities layer and the digital landlords who control scarce powered capacity. Future analysis will examine power generation strategies, chip architecture evolution and its impact on power density, and compute layer economics. But understanding why the facilities layer offers superior risk-adjusted returns requires recognizing how it sits between these other layers: downstream of power generation constraints but upstream of chip and compute obsolescence.

The Power Delivery Bottleneck Is Real

Understanding the magnitude of AI's power demands requires putting the numbers in perspective. A single megawatt (MW) of electricity can power over 600 homes. A 200MW data center, modest by emerging standards, consumes as much electricity as more than 120,000 homes, the number found in a medium-sized city. Hyperscalers are now planning campuses requiring 1-5 gigawatts, equivalent to multiple nuclear power plants.

The demand growth is staggering. Goldman Sachs projects data center power demand will grow 165% by 2030. McKinsey estimates a requirement for over 150 GW to power AI by 2030, representing a 3.5x increase from 2025 levels. There are not currently enough data centers to meet the demand, and shortages will persist for at least the next 5-10 years.

Construction timelines cannot keep pace with demand. It can take 3-5 years to even start building a large data center project. Utility interconnection queues are backed up for years. A new substation to serve a 200MW facility takes 2-3 years to design, permit, and construct. Upgrading transmission lines to handle gigawatt-scale loads can take 5-7 years when accounting for permitting, right-of-way acquisition, and construction. If a site requires new power generation assets such as a natural gas plant (3-4 years), solar farm with storage (2-3 years), or nuclear facility (7-10 years), the timelines extend even further.

This inverts traditional technology constraints. AI companies like OpenAI (OPENAI), Anthropic, and Google are increasingly constrained not by capital, algorithms, or talent, but by the infrastructure lead times required to bring power to their facilities. A hyperscaler can't simply write a check and have gigawatts delivered next quarter. New data centers require years long interconnection studies and power load studies, followed by years of construction.

Geographic constraints amplify this bottleneck. Power-intensive data centers can't simply be built anywhere. They require robust electrical grids, access to cooling resources, favorable regulatory environments, and fiber connectivity. Northern Virginia's "Data Center Alley" hosts roughly 25% of US data centers, in part because Dominion Energy can deliver power at scale. Similar dynamics favor Texas (ERCOT's generation capacity, friendly regulation, and low rates), the Pacific Northwest (hydroelectric abundance and cool climate), and select Midwestern locations.

Companies that control already built facilities or facilities coming online within the next few years hold disproportionate value. This is why bitcoin miners turned AI/HPC facilities providers command premium valuations. They've collapsed years of development timeline into immediate or near-term availability.

Location, Location, Location: The Digital Landlord Model

Companies like Applied Digital, Cipher Mining, and Galaxy Digital are landlords for the AI era. Rather than real estate in fast growing metropolitan areas, they own real estate with large power connections, which are the prime locations for AI. Like landlords everywhere, they lease the location to tenants who desperately need it. The digital landlord model offers two compelling advantages.

Predictable cash flows with minimal technology risk. Like a Manhattan office building owner, AI-powered facilities provide the building and collect rent. They don't need to worry about what their tenants do inside the space, whether training AI models, running inference workloads, or providing cloud services. There's no Moore's Law rendering the building obsolete, no competitive risk from a tenant deciding to develop competing technology, and no execution risk from managing complex compute operations. The core competency (delivering reliable power and cooling) is straightforward and proven.

Tenant desperation creates pricing power. When AI hyperscalers need gigawatts of electricity to train their frontier models, they're like a financial services company looking for an office on Wall Street. They ain’t price shopping. They're securing whatever capacity exists. The seller's market dynamic allows facilities owners to command premium rates and lock in long-term leases that look more like commercial real estate agreements than typical technology contracts. For example, TeraWulf locked in a 10-year lease with 3% annual escalators. Galaxy Digital locked in a 15 year triple-net lease that will net 90% EBITDA margins.

Competing in a Brutal Layer: The Neocloud Model

Neoclouds are companies that provide GPU-as-a-service, providing compute capacity on demand. Operating in the computer layer, neoclouds face fundamentally different risks than AI facilities providers. Three structural headwinds create persistent challenges.

  1. Chip depreciation is relentless. A GPU cluster worth $50 million today may depreciate in 3-5 years to $10 million when new chips are released. This pattern has played out consistently over the history of computing. While Moore's Law doesn't hurt data center buildings, it brutalizes the servers inside them. Neoclouds must continuously buy the latest GPUs to remain competitive. Bitcoin miners faced this same challenge as faster and faster bitcoin mining machines were released every few years, making older machines less valuable. The constant CapEx made Bitcoin mining a low-margin endeavor and presents a possible future for neoclouds.
  2. Hyperscaler competition is existential. Amazon, Microsoft, and Google possess virtually unlimited capital and customers captive to their ecosystems. When a neocloud signs a customer, they're competing against providers who can offer compute alongside storage, databases, machine learning platforms, and enterprise software suites. The hyperscalers can afford to run compute at lower margins or even at cost because they monetize customers across dozens of services. Neoclouds have no such luxury. Compute is their entire business, and hyperscalers can undercut them whenever competitive dynamics demand it.
  3. Margin compression is inevitable as workloads standardize. Early in the AI boom, specialized knowledge about training massive models and optimizing GPU clusters created differentiation. But AI infrastructure is rapidly standardizing. Kubernetes-based orchestration, standard NVIDIA architectures, and increasingly commoditized software stacks mean that one GPU cluster looks much like another. As barriers to entry fall and capacity increases, compute becomes a race to the bottom on price. There may not be a sustainable competitive advantage. The neocloud that wins may simply be whoever can operate at the lowest cost.

Full Stack Neoclouds: Compounding the Risk

If pure neoclouds face structural challenges, full-stack operators, like IREN and Nebius, who provide facilities and neoclouds face both structural challenges and greater execution complexity because they're attempting to excel at two entirely different businesses simultaneously. As digital landlords, they must navigate the grid connection process, secure construction financing, and then build and maintain the AI power facilities. As compute providers, they secure equipment financing, procure and deploy GPU clusters, manage associated workloads, maintain high utilization, and provide customer technical support.

These represent fundamentally different skill sets. Facilities development requires grid and utility relationships, infrastructure and data center construction management, and real estate expertise. Compute operations require hardware and software optimization, distributed systems knowledge, and enterprise sales ability. The disparate nature of the endeavors creates execution risk that compounds across both domains.

Further, the capital intensive nature of both businesses amplifies these challenges. A full stack neocloud must fund both infrastructure (buildings, power systems, cooling) and equipment (GPU clusters). This requires hundreds of millions to billions in upfront capital before generating meaningful revenues. In contrast, pure facilities plays can build out powered space and let tenants bring their own equipment. Pure neoclouds can lease space from multiple landlords, avoiding the capital burden and timeline constraints of building facilities. Full stack neoclouds bear both the real estate development risk and the chip depreciation risk.

Asset Longevity: Buildings vs. Servers

Perhaps the most underappreciated distinction between facilities plays and neoclouds mirrors the difference between owning real estate versus owning the computers inside it.

Facilities infrastructure, such as the buildings, power delivery systems, and cooling infrastructure, operates for 30-50 years. A properly built data center constructed today will still be delivering power and cooling in 2070, long after the current AI boom has cycled through multiple chip generations. Digital Realty (DLR) has had facilities operating since the 2000s, continuously generating cash flows as tenants refresh their equipment inside.

GPU clusters face a 3-6 year depreciation cycle or risk being obsolete. They face massive ongoing reinvestment to stay competitive. AWS constantly refreshes its hardware and structures its business to amortize that refresh cost across millions of customers and dozens of services. Neoclouds do not yet have such ability.

This difference in depreciation timelines makes neoclouds massively more risky. Digital landlords build facilities once and collect rent for decades. Neoclouds must continuously purchase newer chips to stay competitive. As a result, facilities plays operate more like REITs with predictable cash flows and long-lived assets. Neoclouds operate more like technology companies with continuous reinvestment requirements and shorter planning horizons.

Further, the financing implications of the two models are enormous. Banks will lend against data center real estate at attractive rates for 20-year terms because infrastructure retains value over time. AI infrastructure landlords can leverage debt more cheaply and for longer durations than neoclouds, who must finance rapidly depreciating hardware with more expensive, shorter-term debt or equity dilution. This financing advantage allows pure facilities plays to operate with higher leverage and lower costs of capital, just like a REIT.

The Counter-Arguments

Despite being inherently more risky, neoclouds also offer the opportunity for higher rewards.

Margin capture potential. Neoclouds potentially capture more value per megawatt than pure landlords. A company that operates GPU clusters may generate 3-4 times the revenue per kilowatt-hour compared to an AI infrastructure facilities provider. Granted, this may be offset by higher capital expenditures from replacing chips.

Technical differentiation opportunities. Some neoclouds may create sustainable advantages through proprietary support software, custom rack and cooling solutions, or specialized infrastructure for specific AI applications. For example, a company like Nebius, with its team of skilled software and AI engineers, might build a defensible moat through technical excellence.

Operating leverage in constrained markets. During periods of extreme GPU scarcity, neoclouds with deployed capacity can charge premium rates that pure facilities providers cannot capture. If AI demand remains supply-constrained for years, the compute operator role might generate outsized returns that more than compensate for the structural challenges. .

Customer stickiness through integration. Vertically integrated neoclouds might create deeper customer relationships by controlling the entire stack from power through APIs. Customers who build on a neocloud's platform, integrate with their tools, and optimize for their infrastructure might face switching costs that create lock-in. Pure facilities plays, offering commodity powered space, may face greater tenant churn.

These counterarguments aren't dismissible. In scenarios where everything goes right, neoclouds will indeed generate spectacular absolute returns. However, the predictable cash flows of the digital landlords offer a safer way to play the AI infrastructure buildout.

The Next Decade: Persistent Power Scarcity

The power delivery bottleneck shows no signs of easing before the early-to-mid 2030s for several reasons.

Demand growth is accelerating, not slowing. Each new generation of AI models requires more and more compute to train. GPT-3 used roughly 10,000 GPUs, GPT-4 approximately 25,000, and GPT-5 over 100,000. The hyperscalers are in an arms race where falling behind in compute capacity means falling behind in AI capabilities. The result is nearly insatiable demand for powered data center space for the foreseeable future.

Supply constraints are structural and timeline-driven. Even in regions with available grid capacity, connecting power to new facilities requires multi-year processes. Utility interconnection studies take 12-24 months. Load evaluations add another 12-36 months. Lead time for some equipment is 12-24 months. Substation construction takes 18-24 months. Grid generation upgrades can require 5-7 years. These timelines are not easy to execute concurrently, as they involve coordination across utilities, regulators, equipment manufacturers, and construction contractors.

New power generation won't ease facilities constraints until the 2030s. Small modular reactors remain in development and regulatory approval phases. Traditional nuclear plants face decade-long development timelines even with expedited permitting. Natural gas plants can be built faster but face environmental opposition, fuel supply considerations, and growing pressure to reduce carbon emissions. Renewable energy is scaling rapidly but requires storage solutions and significant overbuild to provide the 24/7 reliability that data centers demand. And critically, even once power generation comes online, connecting it to facilities where hyperscalers need it adds years to the timeline.

These infrastructure build constraints create a decade-long window where facilities with delivered power capacity remain scarce and pure facilities plays operate in a structurally favorable environment. By the time infrastructure catches up to demand in the mid-2030s, companies like Applied Digital, Cipher, Core Scientific, Galaxy, and TeraWulf will have generated a decade of cash flows with long-term tenants locked in to decade-long commitments.

The parallel to digital REITs in the early 2000s is appropriate. As enterprises migrated to the cloud and the internet exploded, companies that owned powered data center space in key markets generated outsized returns as digital landlords due to structural shortages. Equinix, Digital Realty, and CoreSite delivered compound annual returns exceeding 20% for years. During that time, owning the scarce facilities was as profitable in many cases as operating the technology inside them. The AI infrastructure build-out is creating a similar dynamic.

Conclusion: Digital Landlords Beat Neocloud Operators

The investment case ultimately comes down to a choice between controlling scarce infrastructure versus competing in a technology layer. Neoclouds offer the promise of capturing the more valuable component of the compute value chain, resulting in higher revenue per megawatt. Pure facilities plays offer the proven economics of being a landlord during a decade-long shortage of powered space.

In growth bull markets, landlords rarely capture the imagination. It’s all about the latest technology and trying to capture the emerging winners. But in infrastructure build-outs—and make no mistake, AI represents an infrastructure build-out as much as a technology revolution—the landlord model consistently generates superior risk-adjusted returns.

Infrastructure timelines are the fundamental bottleneck. No amount of cutting-edge chip design or new AI architecture matters if you don’t have a powered data center. While neoclouds must navigate chip depreciation, hyperscaler competition, and margin compression, facilities providers simply need to be competent landlords.

The capital efficiency advantages compound over time. Buildings appreciate or hold value, while GPUs depreciate. Landlords finance facilities with long-term debt at low rates, while neoclouds struggle to finance rapidly depreciating equipment. Facilities infrastructure generates predictable cash flows supporting enduring REIT-like valuations, while neoclouds must constantly grow or fall behind.

For investors seeking exposure to AI infrastructure, buy the landlords. They offer a clearer path to sustainable, predictable cash flows over the next 10-15 years. Neoclouds may generate spectacular returns if they establish a true competitive advantage, but in investing, it’s often slow and steady who wins the race.

BUY: Applied Digital, Cipher Mining, Galaxy Digital, Core Scientific, and TeraWulf.

data.title: TMC the metals company: Fully Valued At Present I Think
data.description:
Junior Miners are different

A junior miner is normally defined as a would-be mining company that is not yet producing. It could be that the deposit isn't yet properly defined, the technology not fully tested, or even just licenses and permissions are not as yet in place. The whole game with junior miners is a little different from other companies.

For, with such a junior miner, there are varied potential failure points. Each piece of work is to get the company (or deposit, technology, or license) to that next potential failure point. The usual financing method is to finance that test. If the test is failed, then that's that. If passed, then more money is raised to try to overcome that next potential failure point.

All through this process - however many times it needs to be done for the specific company - the money comes from shareholders. So, raise some money with a bit of stock dilution, work to get to the next testing point, succeed, raise some more, and so on. This is just how this industry works.

Keep this in mind.

TMC the metals company Inc (TMC)

I have said here before - a number of times - that I like the basic idea here at The Metals Company. I've been saying it for years in fact. Or, if I'm to be wholly accurate about this, I've been agreeing with the basic mineralogy and then thinking that perhaps they weren't going to be able to pass that next potential failure point. Which, so far at least, they have done.

My view at the moment is that - as I said in that last link there - their future is dependent upon politics. Will they be able to negotiate through international and national politics to be granted a license to do what it seems they are capable of doing.

This never was about mineralogy, that was always sound. It was about the lifting cost from 5 km down on the seabed, they passed that test. Then and now it's about licenses, the legal ability to do what they wish to do.

Given the current politics around the Trump Administration and critical minerals, I'm somewhat surprised that they've not already been issued that license, to be honest.

However, my view of the company as an investment at current prices is that it is currently fully priced. There might well be fun and games to be had with price movements - which I would class more as speculation than investing - but as a logical proposition, I would suggest this is a fair price currently.

To repeat earlier points

These are explained in more detail in those earlier pieces linked above. The metal contents of those manganese nodules are well known. How to process them is equally well known. They can be fed into a standard process already used for copper/nickel/cobalt processing. They even have a linkup with Glencore, which owns such processing factories.

Assuming TMC can land those nodules, then they can, definitively, be processed into useful metals that TMC will get paid for.

My worry was initially that the simple costs of lifting material that distance - from the deep seabed to the surface - would be too expensive. This is something underappreciated outside mining, I think, but well known within it. The problem with really deep, deep mines (say, some gold mines in South Africa) is the sheer energy cost of lifting ore to the surface.

TMC has demonstrated, to my satisfaction at least, that they can deal with this.

So, the nodules are there, they contain valuable metals, they can be processed, and they can be lifted.

All that remains is gaining the licenses to allow this to happen.

Licenses

In a logical world there would be no problem here either. Lifting nodules off the sea bottom is clearly less environmentally damaging than blasting millions of tonnes of rock to extract lower-grade ore. But that's to miss politics. The environmentalists (I occasionally have the joy of reading Greenpeace and the like reports about how awful it would be to mine the sea bottom) have decided this is a bad thing.

There is an international process going on to decide upon the terms under which such licenses should/would be granted. The international bureaucracy doesn't seem to be putting itself under any pressure to issue those licenses in a timely manner - to be mild about it. There's also a potential option of the US itself issuing such a license - what TMC is currently gunning for. They might get it too.

Company finances

The recent financial figures will make uncomfortable reading to some.

Ooooh! Negative equity and.....no, this is normal enough in junior miners. We all know this is high risk - that task of getting to the next failure point - and that's not the part that we worry about. Rather, it's the cash line that we do. Do they have enough cash to get to that next possible failure point? Because, as above, if they pass it then the company is worth more, and we all know that's when they're going to have an equity issue. Because that's how junior miners work.

That cash line there looks fine. Except:

Oooh, ouch. General and admin costs are up about 5x from last year. And if we go at that last quarter's run rate, we're no more than 6 months from effectively running out of cash. Which would mean a dilutive stock issuance without there having been that passing of a potential failure point and risk reduction. Those are always painful.

Except. What that bounce in this most recent quarter is that management options (or restricted stock, makes no matter) have matured and vested.

There's also some concern expressed out there that said management, insiders, have been selling stock. This is normal. When management stock vests, that's the trigger for their tax bill. We would prefer that management did not go bust trying to pay their taxes on their stock vesting. So, it's normal that management gets to sell enough of their vested stock to pay their tax bill.

Oh, sure, if management sells all of their vested stock, then that's something we would raise an eyebrow at. But selling enough of their new fortune to pay their taxes - maybe plus a fancy car and a beach house, you know, a bit more - is wholly normal. I will not claim to have done the full green-eyeshade analysis of the size of those stock sales here, but a quick eyeball leads me to tax bills plus perhaps some toys, not to a cashing out of the position.

So, where are we then?

Once we accept that junior mining companies are always running on enough cash to get to the next failure point, correct for stock vestings and taxes to be paid, then TMC is doing just fine. By those standards of junior miners, that is.

The whole thing is dependent upon the grant of a license - an exploitation license. Perhaps the Law of the Sea, UN-led process, will come to some conclusion. Assuming that the international process ever does reach a conclusion on the matter. Perhaps one will be issued by the US Government.

And, well, there, I just don't know. For I do know a couple of people in this process - not the TMC decision, no, I don't mean that, rather the admin's whole critical minerals policy - and I'm even related to someone on the fringes of it. And I do not understand their decision making process in the slightest. To me, here with TMC, the correct decision is issue the license and let it rip. This isn't what is being done, and I don't know why because I don't understand how they are weighing their decisions.

But even so

I don't know whether the license will be granted. But we still need to come up with a valuation. And my opinion is that TMC looks fairly, if not fully, valued even if the license is granted. From elsewhere:

A Pre‑Feasibility Study for the NORI‑D project that estimated a net present value of about $5.5 billion and declared 51 million tonnes of probable mineral reserves for deep‑sea polymetallic nodules—described as a world‑first for this type of resource. An Initial Assessment for the rest of its NORI and TOML areas, putting their NPV around $18.1 billion, for a combined NPV of $23.6 billion across the portfolio

And, well, TMC is valued at $3 billion or so right now. PFS value of $6 billion (just to keep the math easy) discounted by 50%? Seems reasonable to me. Initial assessment numbers? No, those are to be discounted much more heavily than that.

And, do not forget, those numbers are assuming that the mining - exploitation - license is granted.

One more thing

It is a firm conviction of mine that mining a new mine is different from having a new mining technique or technology. Owning a mountain of copper ore, well, you own a mountain of copper ore. On the other hand, if you have a new method of extracting copper from a mountain, that's still good. But once you are then mining copper, then everyone else knows this new method is viable. So, they'll reverse engineer this method and compete with you. As actually did happen when the SX/EW method was applied to mining copper, instead of gaining copper from sulfite deposits, we could do so from oxide deposits.

So, my expectation is that TMC assumes it gains a license, then shows that mining nodules is profitable. Then there will be considerable competition, and the first-mover advantage disappears. There is, after all, a lot of seabed out there and many, many nodules upon it.

Patents, well, yes. Those are going to survive if - say, and just as an example - China says it wants to reverse engineer the technology. Right?

So, I'm willing to run with that first iteration of NPV being viable, but that second and subsequent one, well, I think - at very best - the discount rate to be applied to it should be considerable.

Why I'm wrong

It is, of course, possible that TMC gets granted an exploitation license under US law. Further, that the next administration doesn't just cancel it. This would depend upon who the next admin is, which is one of those problems with investing based upon politics.

Given what we know about TMC so far, I would expect that to be profitable. Removing the uncertainty will make TMC worth more.

My opinion

My opinion actually is that TMC is pretty fully valued even with the granting of such a license.

Yes, yes, I know, people who own vast deposits of minerals can be worth tens of billions of $ and TMC is currently only worth low single digits of billions of $.

But the point of TMC is not, in fact, ownership of vast deposits. It's the ability to exploit those deposits we know are out there, many of which are owned by other people - or, in fact, given maritime law, owned by no one. Once the proof of exploitability happens, I expect substantial competition.

I have no doubt - none at all - that there will be fun times to be had trading in TMC stock. I would expect a substantial rise - followed by a stock issue, of course - in the stock price if an exploitation license is issued by whoever, the US or the UN.

But a permanent and higher stock price? No, I don't think so. I think - please do note opinion here - that TMC is currently about fairly valued.

That is, TMC is a trading stock, not an investment one.

data.title: Whale's Insight: Into Core Market Infrastructure
data.description:
• The CFTC’s new pilot program allows select firms to use BTC (BTC-USD), ETH (ETH-USD), and USDC (USDC-USD) as regulated collateral in U.S. derivatives markets, a shift that is expected to create a new steady source of demand for BTC and ETH.

  • The core financial infrastructure provider DTCC has received SEC approval to launch a tokenization service that will allow U.S. securities to be issued on approved blockchains with full legal rights and protections.

  • Traditional finance firms are beginning to roll out more specialized Bitcoin-linked products, such as JPMorgan’s structured IBIT note and the proposed AfterDark ETF.

CFTC Opens Door to Digital Asset Collateral in Derivatives Markets

The U.S. Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC) has launched a pilot program that, for the first time, allows bitcoin, ether, and certain stablecoins such as USDC to be posted as collateral in U.S. derivatives markets.

The pilot is limited to futures commission merchants that meet specific eligibility standards. Participating firms may accept approved digital assets as margin for futures and swaps but must adhere to heightened operational requirements. For the first three months, weekly disclosures on digital asset holdings are mandatory, along with immediate notification to the CFTC if any custody or risk issues arise.

In practice, this could mean a registered firm accepting bitcoin as collateral for a leveraged swap tied to commodities, while the CFTC monitors the operational risks and custody arrangements behind the scenes.

Key Take

  • In derivative markets, trading firms and institutions need to post collateral to back their positions in futures, swaps, or other derivative instruments. They normally maintain sizable reserves of assets that regulators deem safe and liquid enough to serve as collateral, typically U.S. Treasuries or cash-like instruments.

  • Bitcoin can now be accepted as a margin for futures and swaps. This creates direct incentives for institutions, including hedge funds, market makers, and FCMs, to maintain meaningful BTC balances. They will need readily accessible Bitcoin reserves to maintain trading flexibility, like meeting margin calls and managing intraday exposure.

  • As institutions begin using bitcoin as collateral, this could create a new source of steady demand, reduce available float in spot markets, and further entrench bitcoin’s position as a mainstream, regulated financial instrument rather than a purely trading asset.

DTCC Receives SEC Approval to Tokenize U.S. Securities

The Depository Trust & Clearing Corporation (DTCC), the core post-trade infrastructure behind U.S. securities markets, has received a No-Action Letter from the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission. The No-Action Letter authorizes the company to launch a controlled production-level tokenization service beginning in the second half of 2026.

The approval allows DTC, a subsidiary of DTCC, to create tokenized versions of assets it already custodies, including securities in the Russell 1000, major index-tracking ETFs, and U.S. Treasuries, on pre-approved blockchains over a three-year period. These digital representations will carry the same legal rights, investor protections, and ownership entitlements as their traditional counterparts, while operating within DTCC’s established standards for safety and resilience.

This is the first time the SEC has permitted a systemically important market structure company to tokenize core U.S. securities in a live environment. DTCC positions the initiative as a foundation for improving collateral mobility, enabling around-the-clock access, and supporting programmable asset workflows.

Key Take

  • DTCC is the central post-trade infrastructure for U.S. securities markets, responsible for clearing, settlement, and custody for the majority of equities and bonds traded in the United States. It is one of the most systemically important financial institutions in global markets.

  • Under today’s broker-mediated system, DTCC holds securities in centralized custody while shareholder rights, such as voting and corporate action participation, flow through brokers to investors; DTCC’s role ensures the integrity of ownership records and the proper transmission of these rights across the market infrastructure.

  • Most tokenized stocks today offer only price exposure and do not grant shareholder rights, but DTCC’s new tokenization framework is designed to issue digital representations that carry the full entitlements, investor protections, and ownership rights of traditional securities.

New Bitcoin-Linked Financial Products Emerge

In recent weeks, several traditional finance firms have introduced new Bitcoin-linked products, signaling that interest is now moving beyond straightforward spot ETFs.

JPMorgan has filed to issue a structured note tied to BlackRock’s iShares Bitcoin Trust (IBIT). The product outlines several predefined payout scenarios: investors receive a fixed return if the ETF reaches a target price by 2026, while a separate payoff structure offers upside participation through 2028 with partial downside protection.

Separately, the Nicholas Bitcoin and Treasuries AfterDark ETF has been submitted for SEC review. The fund proposes a time-based allocation model: during daytime trading hours, it would hold U.S. Treasuries, shifting into Bitcoin exposure overnight. The structure aims to capture periods when Bitcoin historically shows higher volatility outside of U.S. market hours.

These products show how traditional institutions are experimenting with different ways to package Bitcoin exposure, using structured payoffs and time-based allocation models to appeal to a wider range of investor preferences.

Key Take

  • For most investors, a straightforward spot ETF is sufficient to gain Bitcoin exposure. However, this does not meet the needs of many sophisticated market participants. The structured note market in the US has developed very rapidly in recent years, driven by investor demand for customized risk-reward profiles. These products can be designed to match specific market views, offering flexibility and risk mitigation strategies that align with sophisticated investment goals.

  • The JPMorgan's IBIT-linked note is designed for investors who want exposure to Bitcoin’s upside but require guardrails around volatility and drawdowns. It delivers a structured, asymmetric payoff: modest guaranteed return in the near term, leveraged upside long term, and a defined drawdown risk in 2028.

  • Bitcoin trades 24/7, but its performance is not uniform throughout the day; in this cycle, and one notable phenomenon is that most of the gains have occurred outside the U.S. market hours. The AfterDark ETF is designed for investors who want exposure specifically during the periods when Bitcoin has historically shown the most movement, rather than holding the asset around the clock.

Weekly Market Chart: Hyperliquid Dominates Blockchain Fee Capture

According to data from The Block, Hyperliquid now captures roughly 50% of all blockchain fees, a steep climb from about 10% at the beginning of the year. This growth has come largely at the expense of Ethereum and Solana. Together, those two networks now represent only about 20% of the total fee share, down from more than half at the start of 2025.

As we mentioned in our recent article, Whale’s Digital Asset View: Top Two at a Crossroad, emerging top decentralized applications this cycle, including Hyperliquid for perps and Polymarket in prediction markets, are not built on Ethereum. While Ethereum is a more decentralized network, and that is more attractive for large capital store assets for safety reasons, its high TVL does not directly lead to more economic activity or fee generation, challenging the value accrual thesis for ETH as an asset.

Disclaimer: The information provided herein does not constitute investment advice, financial advice, trading advice, or any other sort of advice, and should not be treated as such. All content set out below is for informational purposes only.

Original Post

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Calamos Convertible And High Income Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Overview

The third quarter marked a pivotal shift in the monetary policy landscape as the Federal Reserve initiated its first rate cut of 2025, lowering the federal funds rate by 25 basis points. While modest in isolation, this move signals the central bank's transition toward a less restrictive monetary stance, with the potential for additional cuts before year-end. Additionally, US economic fundamentals remain solid, underpinned by pro-growth fiscal policies and continued corporate investment in transformative technologies such as artificial intelligence (AI). Although inflation remains somewhat elevated, powerful disinflationary forces from AI-driven productivity gains provide a constructive counterbalance to tariff-related price pressures.

This environment proved supportive for risk assets, with equity markets delivering solid returns and credit spreads continuing their compression from earlier-year highs. It also provided a tailwind for the convertible market, where issuers tend to be small- and mid-cap focused companies. This cohort stands to benefit as equity market leadership broadens beyond mega-cap technology stocks—a development we believe represents a significant opportunity, supported by both fundamental momentum and the historical benefits of rate-cutting cycles for these types of companies.

The strong equity market performance in the third quarter contributed to convertibles with the most equity sensitivity (+11.5%) outperform convertibles with total return attributes (+10.4%) and those with the most bond sensitivity (+5.1%). At the end of the quarter, convertibles with total return attributes (39% of the market) exceeded convertibles with the most bond sensitivity (35%) and exceeded those with equity sensitive attributes (26%).

All economic sectors within the US convertible index delivered positive results in the third quarter. The sectors which performed best during the quarter included materials (+50.6%), real estate (+21.3%) and industrials (+21.2%). Those sectors which lagged the convertible index the most included consumer staples (+1.0%), communication services (+3.0%) and financials (+3.9%).

Non-rated convertibles (+10.1%) outperformed speculative grade rated (+5.7%) and investment grade rated (+4.6%) issues. Roughly 79% of the US convertible market was unrated on average at the end of the third quarter according to data provided by ICE BofA.

Global new convertible issuance was exceptionally robust, with Q3's $54.4 billion representing one of the strongest third-quarter volumes in recent memory and bringing year-to-date totals above $125 billion. This strength reflects companies' strategic use of convertibles as a cost-effective tool for capitalizing on growth opportunities as well as refinancing other forms of debt. We expect this favorable issuance environment to persist into year-end and beyond.

The US high yield bond market, as represented by the Bloomberg Barclays US Corporate High Yield 2% Issuer Capped Index returned +2.54% in the third quarter. High-yield spreads closed the quarter 23 basis points tighter at +267. Investment grade spreads were also tighter over the period, closing at +74 basis points following the previous quarter close of +83. Driven by spread compression and recovery in a few larger capital structures, CCC issuers led all rating category returns at +4.4%. BBs and B-rated paper each returned 2.3% with limited dispersion among issuers. Trailing twelve-month defaults on high yield bonds remained at 1.4% in September, well below the long-term average of 3.4%. Leveraged loan defaults were at 3.5%, compared with the long-term average of 3.1%. The best-performing sectors in the Bloomberg Barclays US Corporate High Yield 2% Issuer Cap Index were natural gas utilities (+4.9%), communications (+3.6%) and energy (+2.9%), while technology (+1.4%), basic industry (+1.9%) and capital goods (+1.9%) represented the largest laggards.

Distributions During the Period Remained Competitive

The fund's current annualized distribution rate was 10.70% of the market price as of September 30, 2025. We believe the fund's monthly distributions are highly competitive. For example, the ICE BofA All US Convertibles Index had a yield of 1.94%, the 10-year US Treasury bond yield was 4.16%, and the ICE BofA US High Yield Index effective yield was 6.56% as of September 30, 2025.

Performance Review

For the quarter ending March 31, 2025, the fund advanced 4.91% on price and +9.97% on NAV. The NAV return finished above the blended comparator index (50% ICE BofA All US Convertibles Index, 50% Bloomberg US HY 2% Issuer Capped Index), which returned 5.53% for the same period.

Contributing Factors. Relative to the comparator index, the fund benefitted from security selection and an average overweight stance in information technology, as holdings in technology hardware, storage & peripherals, application software, and communications equipment added to relative returns. Additionally, favorable security selection in consumer discretionary added to the fund's performance. The main contributors within this sector were broadline retail and homefurnishing retail.

Detracting Factors. Relative to the comparator index, security selection within the financial sector negatively affected performance where holdings in the consumer finance, asset management & custody banks, and transaction & payment processing services industries lagged. In addition, an underweight position and security selection in real estate, specifically in the real estate services and data center REITs industries, lagged on a relative basis.

Positioning and Portfolio Changes

As of quarter end, convertible securities account for approximately 69% of our investments, whereas corporate bonds account for around 24% of the portfolio. At approximately 61%, unrated securities comprise the largest rating class—as most convertibles are unrated—though we perform our own credit analysis. We continue to take a selective approach to CCC credits, representing approximately 1% of the portfolio at quarter end. We hold a large percentage in the BB-credit tier (approximately 15%), which offers attractive valuations and provides access to a large portion of the convertible bond universe where we can optimize returns through our proprietary research capabilities. At quarter end, the weighted average duration of our bond holdings is 2.4 years.

Regarding economic sectors, the largest portfolio weights are in information technology and consumer discretionary on an absolute basis. Conversely, consumer staples and real estate represent the smallest absolute sector weights with holdings. We maintain overweight allocations to the application software and systems software industries. Cable & satellite and asset management & custody banks constitute the most significant underweight industries.

Allocations to information technology and energy rose during the period with increased weights in systems software and coal & consumable fuels. The communication services allocation decreased during the period, while the weight to health care also decreased modestly.

Leverage

We believe the current economic environment is conducive to the prudent use of leverage to enhance total return and support the fund's distribution rate. As of September 30, 2025, our total percentage of assets leveraged was approximately 34%.

Outlook

Looking ahead, we anticipate continued pro-growth fiscal policies combined with a data-dependent but increasingly accommodative monetary policy framework. Markets have advanced strongly from the tariff-induced concerns earlier in the year and may experience periods of volatility into year-end as investors debate the magnitude of recent gains. Nevertheless, we believe risk assets can continue to benefit from strong fundamental momentum across our core investment themes.

The hybrid structure of convertible securities—combining equity-like growth potential with bond-like risk-mitigation characteristics—offers an attractive risk-reward proposition in this environment. We believe active management remains essential, as each convertible security offers unique structural features that can evolve over time, making disciplined security selection and ongoing portfolio management critical to optimizing return profiles.

When the net investment income and net realized capital gains are not sufficient, a portion of the level rate distribution will be a return of capital. In addition, a limited number of distributions per calendar year may include net realized long-term capital gains. Distribution rate may vary.

There is no assurance that the Fund will achieve or maintain its investment objective. Please refer to back page for important notes. All returns are in USD terms unless otherwise indicated.

For additional information or to download a fact sheet, please visit the fund's profile page:

CHY - Convertible and High Income Fund | Calamos Investments

Important Fund Information

The goal of the managed distribution policy is to provide investors with a predictable, though not assured, level of cash flow. Monthly distributions paid may include net investment income, net realized short-term capital gains, net realized long-term capital gains and, if necessary, return of capital. Maintenance of this policy may increase transaction and tax costs associated with the fund.

A credit rating is a relative and subjective measure of a bond issuer's credit risk, including the possibility of default. Credit ratings are assigned to companies by third-party groups, such as Standard & Poor's. Assets with the highest ratings are referred to as "investment grade" while those in the lower tiers are referred to as "noninvestment grade" or "high-yield." Ratings are measured using a scale that typically ranges from AAA (higheSt) to D (lowest).

Leverage creates risks which may adversely affect return, including the likelihood of greater volatility of net asset value and market price of common shares; and fluctuations in the variable rates of the leverage financing. The ratio is the percent of total managed assets.

The Fund may invest up to 25% of its assets in foreign securities and invest in an array of security types and market cap sizes, each of which has a unique risk profile. As a result of political or economic instability in foreign countries, there can be special risks associated with investing in foreign securities. These include fluctuations in currency exchange rates, increased price volatility, and difficulty obtaining information.

Investments by the Fund in lower-rated securities involve substantial risk of loss and present greater risks than investments in higher-rated securities, including less liquidity and increased price sensitivity to changing interest rates and to a deteriorating economic environment. Fixed income securities are subject to interest-rate risk; as interest rates go up, the value of debt securities in the Fund's portfolio generally will decline. There are certain risks associated with an investment in a convertible bond such as default risk—that the company issuing a convertible security may be unable to repay principal and interest, and interest rate risk—that the convertible may decrease in value if interest rates increase.

Owning a bond fund is not the same as directly owning fixed income securities. If the market moves, losses will occur instantaneously, and there will be no ability to hold a bond to maturity.

The Fund may invest in derivative securities, including options. The use of derivatives presents risks different from, and possibly greater than, the risks associated with investing directly in traditional securities. There is no assurance that any derivative strategy used by the Fund will succeed. One of the risks associated with purchasing an option is that the Fund pays a premium whether or not the option is exercised. The Fund may seek to purchase index put options to help reduce downside exposure. However, the effectiveness of the fund's index option-based risk management strategy may be reduced if the fund's equity portfolio does not correlate to the performance of the underlying option positions. The Fund also risks losing all or part of the cash paid for purchasing index options. Unusual market conditions or lack of a ready market of any particular option at a specific time may reduce the effectiveness of the fund's option strategies, and for these and other reasons the fund's option strategies may not reduce the fund's volatility to the extent desired. From time to time, the Fund may reduce its holdings of put options, resulting in an increased exposure to a market decline. Please refer to the fund's prospectus for a full description of risks.

Parties entering an interest rate swap take on exposure to a given interest rate; the exposure can be long or short depending on whether a counterparty is paying or receiving the fixed rate. At the same time, each party takes on the risk —known as counterparty credit risk—that the other party will default at some time during the life of the contract.

Terms

A Managed Distribution Policy is an investment company's commitment to common shareholders to provide a predictable, but not assured, level of cash flow. Market Price refers to the price at which shares of the fund trade in the market. NAV or Net Asset Value refers to the net

value of all the assets held in the fund. EBITDA stands for earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization. It is a measure of a company's profitability that excludes the effects of its capital structure, tax strategy, and depreciation methods.

Index Definitions
